blocks.content: Parquet has per-column min/max statistics per row group. But they only help if the data in each row group has a tight value range for your filter column. If every row group contains all possible values of user_country, no row groups can be skipped. Enter Z-ordering: multi-dimensional sorting to cluster related data together.

# Without Z-ordering: random distribution
# └── row_group_0: countries = [US, IN, BR, DE, FR, CN, ...] ← can't skip
# └── row_group_1: countries = [US, IN, BR, DE, FR, CN, ...] ← can't skip

# With ZORDER BY event_date, country:
# └── row_group_0: dates=[Jan,Jan,Jan], countries=[US,US,CA]  ← can skip most queries!
# └── row_group_1: dates=[Jan,Jan,Jan], countries=[DE,FR,GB]  ← skipped for US queries
# └── row_group_2: dates=[Feb,Feb,Feb], countries=[US,IN,BR]  ← skipped for Jan queries

Z-ordering (Z-order curve / Morton curve) is a space-filling curve that maps multi-dimensional coordinates to a single dimension while preserving locality. Data points near each other in N-D space end up near each other in 1-D storage order. This maximizes data skipping when filtering on multiple columns simultaneously.

# Delta OPTIMIZE with ZORDER BY
# (requires Delta Lake — not vanilla Parquet)

# OPTIMIZE rewrites files to be larger (addresses small file problem too)
# ZORDER BY clusters data by the given columns

spark.sql("""
    OPTIMIZE events_delta
    ZORDER BY (event_date, country)
""")

# Or using Python DeltaTable API:
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

dt = DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'events_delta')
dt.optimize().executeZOrderBy('event_date', 'country')

# After Z-ordering, queries like:
#   WHERE event_date = '2024-01-15' AND country = 'US'
# can skip 90%+ of files
Z-ordering is expensive to run (rewrites all files). Run it on a schedule (nightly/weekly) on partitions that are no longer receiving new data, not on every write.

sections.title: Bloom Filters: The Complement to Z-Ordering

blocks.content: Z-ordering works well for range queries. For point lookups on high-cardinality columns (e.g., WHERE user_id = 'a8f3c...'), bloom filters are more efficient. A bloom filter is stored per column per row group and answers: 'Does this row group definitely NOT contain this value?'

# Enable bloom filters on a Delta table column
spark.sql("""
    ALTER TABLE events_delta
    SET TBLPROPERTIES (
        'delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols' = 5,
        'delta.bloomFilter.columns' = 'user_id,session_id'
    )
""")

# After OPTIMIZE, Spark generates bloom filter indexes per file
# Point lookups: WHERE user_id = 'abc123'
# → Spark checks bloom filter → if negative, skips file entirely
# False positive rate ~1% by default (configurable)

Z-ordering limitations: (1) Effectiveness degrades with more than 3-4 columns — the curve spreads data too broadly. (2) New data written after OPTIMIZE is not Z-ordered — re-run periodically. (3) Doesn't help with aggregations that read all data anyway. (4) Works only with Delta Lake, not vanilla Parquet.

rows: ZORDER BY + partition by coarse grain (year/month)
New streaming data not yet compacted
Run OPTIMIZE on completed partitions nightly

rune_cards.definition: Space-filling curve (Morton code) that interleaves the bits of multi-dimensional coordinates to produce a 1-D sort order. Data points near each other in N dimensions cluster together in 1-D storage, maximizing file-level data skipping.

Delta Lake command that rewrites small files into larger, optimally-sized files (bin-packing). Optionally applies ZORDER BY for data clustering. 'OPTIMIZE my_table ZORDER BY (col1, col2)' does both in one operation.

Spark reads Delta's min/max column statistics from the transaction log (not the Parquet footer) to determine which files can possibly satisfy a filter. Files where max < filter_value or min > filter_value are skipped without being opened.

Probabilistic data structure that quickly answers 'definitely not in this file' for point lookups. No false negatives (if value is in file, filter says yes). ~1% false positives (says yes but value isn't there). Complements Z-ordering for high-cardinality ID lookups.

quiz.question: You have a Delta table queried mostly with WHERE product_id = 'P12345' (point lookups on a UUID-like 10M distinct IDs). Should you ZORDER BY product_id or use bloom filters?
options: ZORDER BY product_id — it's always better
Bloom filters — Z-ordering is poor at point lookups on high-cardinality columns
Partition by product_id first, then ZORDER
Neither — point lookups don't benefit from any indexing in Spark
quiz.feedback: Z-ordering is designed for range queries on correlated columns. For point lookups (equality filters) on high-cardinality columns, bloom filters answer 'does this file contain product_id P12345?' much more efficiently. Z-ordering 10M UUIDs provides negligible locality benefit.

quiz.question: You ZORDER your table by (event_date, country). How often should you re-run OPTIMIZE ZORDER?
options: On every write — so all data is always Z-ordered
Periodically (nightly/weekly) on partitions that are no longer receiving new data
quiz.feedback: New data written after OPTIMIZE is not Z-ordered — it arrives as new small files. Re-run OPTIMIZE on 'closed' partitions (past dates) on a schedule. Running on every write is too expensive; running once means new data is never ordered.
